title: Empty the head onto the page
subtitle: A five-prompt set for the night the head won't switch off.
excerpt: A five-prompt journal set for the wired evening, the body in bed but the mind still running through the day, tomorrow, and the day after. Two warm-up prompts to slow the head, three narrower prompts to empty it. Twenty-five minutes if you write the full set; shorter is fine.
sourceNotes: Bedtime free-writing is public-domain practice, traceable through diaristic traditions across the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, popularised in modern form by Julia Cameron's morning-pages work (The Artist's Way, 1992). Theme adapted from Day 2 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Calm the racing mind".

content.text: A five-prompt journal set for the wired evening. Body tired, mind still running, through the day's residue, tomorrow's calendar, the conversation that's still unfinished. Five prompts: two warm-up, three narrower. Twenty-five minutes if you write the full set, five minutes per prompt against a timer; the practice still works if you only get through the warm-ups.
New to this style of journal practice? Read
journal prompts as practice
first.
The prompts
Warm-up
1. What is in my head right now?
Just list. The half-thoughts, the half-worries, the half-plans. No structure, no priority. Five minutes against a timer.
2. Which one is loudest?
Read back the list. Circle or note the one that pulls hardest. The loudest one is the one to write into. Five minutes.
Closer in
3. What is the worry underneath the worry?
Take the loudest thought from prompt 2 and ask: if this thing went well, what would the next worry be? Often the surface worry is masking a quieter underneath one. Five minutes.
4. What can I write down so my head doesn't have to hold it tonight?
The to-do items, the people to message, the things to remember. Get them out of the head and onto the page. The page can hold them through the night; the head doesn't have to. Five minutes.
5. What is one true thing I know about tonight?

Not aspirational; true. "The door is locked." "The children are in bed." "I have got through every night so far." One sentence. Five minutes if you want to expand; one minute if the sentence is enough.
After the prompts
Close the notebook. Take one slow breath. The act of writing is the practice; you don't have to do anything with what you wrote. Most of the page will stay on the page and the head will let it go.
If a line in your writing made you emotional, that's the line to come back to tomorrow. Mark the corner of the page with a pencil dot so you can find it; don't re-read it tonight.
